original_abstract: This paper introduces an innovative collaborative game designed to crowdsource natural language referring expressions. By employing a two-player framework, we are able to simultaneously collect and verify expressions in a single seamless process. To date, this approach has yielded a large-scale dataset comprising 130,525 expressions for 96,654 distinct objects across 19,894 photographs of complex natural scenes. This dataset significantly surpasses previous Referring Expression Generation (REG) corpora in both scale and linguistic diversity, facilitating a more robust study of how humans describe objects in real-world contexts. We provide a comprehensive analysis of the collected data and, leveraging these insights, propose a novel optimization-based model for generating referring expressions. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experimental evaluations across three diverse test sets.
humanized_abstract: In this paper, we propose a collaborative game to crowdsource natural language referring expressions for objects in images. Our two-player game format achieves the unique benefit of producing referring expressions and verifying the corresponding objects at the same time. We evaluate the collected expressions and demonstrate that the resulting CrowRef corpus of 130,525 expressions for 96,654 objects in 19,894 images of varying complex natural scenes is not only much larger but also more diverse than existing REG corpora. We present a detailed study of the CrowRef corpus, which sheds light on the object descriptions used by humans when referring to visual objects in images. Based on our findings, we formulate an optimization-based approach to referring expression generation. We conduct experiments on three datasets and show the merits of our approach.

We present a detailed study of the CrowRef corpus, which sheds light on the object descriptions used by humans when referring to visual objects in images. Based on our findings, we formulate an optimization-based approach to referring expression generation. We conduct experiments on three datasets and show the merits of our approach.
